name: Observability & Monitoring
description: Metrics, logs, traces, Prometheus, Grafana, alerting and SLOs — how you know production is healthy.

questions.text: The three classic "pillars" of observability are…
options: backups, patching and antivirus scanning
metrics, logs and traces
uptime, downtime and maintenance windows
CPU, memory and disk utilisation
questions.explanation: Metrics (numbers over time), logs (event records) and traces (request journeys) are the three complementary signal types.

questions.text: Monitoring vs observability — the usual distinction is that observability…
options: is just the new marketing name for monitoring
only applies to cloud-hosted applications
lets you ask new questions of a system, not just check known conditions
replaces the need for any alerting, dashboards or on-call rotas entirely
questions.explanation: Monitoring checks known failure modes; observability means the system emits enough signal to investigate questions you didn't anticipate.

questions.text: In Prometheus, a counter metric…
options: only ever goes up (until a process restart resets it)
goes up and down freely to track the current value of a quantity
stores a fixed set of percentile values
records one free-text event per increment
questions.explanation: Counters are cumulative and monotonic — you derive useful values like per-second rates from them with rate().

questions.text: A gauge differs from a counter because a gauge…
options: can only be scraped once per minute
must always be expressed as a percentage of some fixed maximum value
is reserved for hardware sensors only
represents a value that can rise and fall, like memory in use
questions.explanation: Gauges snapshot a current level (memory, queue depth, temperature); counters accumulate totals.

questions.text: Prometheus collects metrics primarily by…
options: receiving pushes initiated by every application it monitors
scraping HTTP endpoints that expose them (pull model)
reading application log files from disk
polling the cloud provider's billing API
questions.explanation: Prometheus pulls from /metrics endpoints on a schedule; push exists only via the Pushgateway for special cases like short-lived jobs.

questions.text: Grafana's main role in a monitoring stack is to…
options: store the raw time-series data long term
replace the need for a metrics database
visualise data from sources like Prometheus in dashboards
collect metrics directly from instrumented application code
questions.explanation: Grafana is the visualisation and dashboarding layer; storage and collection are done by data sources like Prometheus.

questions.text: Structured logging means…
options: emitting logs as machine-parseable fields (e.g. JSON) rather than free text
writing logs only when an error occurs
keeping every log forever in cold storage
printing logs in a fixed column width so they line up neatly for human readers
questions.explanation: Structured logs (like pino's JSON output) can be filtered and aggregated by field, which free-text lines make painful.

questions.text: Standard log levels ordered from least to most severe are…
options: warn, debug, error, info
error, warn, info, debug
info, debug, warn, error
debug, info, warn, error
questions.explanation: debug < info < warn < error is the conventional ordering; production typically runs at info and above.

questions.text: An SLA differs from an SLO in that an SLA is…
options: an internal stretch goal nobody is told about
a contractual promise to customers, often with penalties
the dashboard where uptime is displayed
the raw measurement that the target is ultimately judged against
questions.explanation: SLI = the measurement, SLO = your internal target for it, SLA = the external contract (usually looser than the SLO).

questions.text: "Alert fatigue" describes the situation where…
options: alert rules consume too much CPU on the server
one alert repeats endlessly because it was never acknowledged
so many noisy alerts fire that real ones get ignored
engineers receive alerts outside working hours
questions.explanation: Noisy, non-actionable alerts train people to ignore pages — the fix is fewer, better alerts tied to real user impact.

questions.text: rate(http_requests_total[5m]) gives you…
options: the per-second average increase of the counter over 5 minutes
the cumulative total of requests served since the process first started
the number of requests in the busiest single second
a prediction of the request volume 5 minutes ahead
questions.explanation: rate() turns a cumulative counter into a per-second rate averaged over the given window — the bread and butter of PromQL.

questions.text: High label cardinality is a problem in Prometheus because…
options: labels beyond the tenth one are silently dropped from every series at scrape time
each unique label combination creates a separate time series, exploding memory
PromQL cannot filter on more than three labels
Grafana refuses to plot more than 100 series
questions.explanation: Every unique label set is its own series — putting user IDs or request paths in labels can melt a Prometheus server.

questions.text: Alerting on symptoms rather than causes means preferring alerts like…
options: "a deployment happened somewhere" over "error rate went up"
"disk at 60%" over "users see errors"
"a pod restarted somewhere" over "latency is high"
"users are getting 5xx errors" over "CPU is at 90%"
questions.explanation: Symptom alerts fire on real user impact; cause-based alerts (CPU high, pod restarted) are often noise with no visible effect.

questions.text: In distributed tracing, a "span" is…
options: the gap in time between two consecutive incidents affecting the same service or host
the retention period of the tracing backend
one timed operation within a trace, with a parent-child relationship to others
the network distance between two services
questions.explanation: A trace is a tree of spans; each span records one operation's timing and metadata, linked by trace and parent IDs.

questions.text: A correlation (or request) ID is useful because it…
options: lets you follow one request's log lines across multiple services
encrypts the log entries end to end
guarantees log lines arrive in order
compresses repeated log messages into a single deduplicated entry
questions.explanation: Propagating one ID through every hop lets you grep the whole journey of a single request across service boundaries.

questions.text: The "golden signals" of monitoring are…
options: commits, builds, deployments and rollbacks
latency, traffic, errors and saturation
uptime, cost, capacity and headcount
CPU, memory, disk and network
questions.explanation: Google's SRE book proposes latency, traffic, errors and saturation as the four signals worth watching for any user-facing system.

questions.text: Why prefer p95/p99 latency over the average?
options: percentiles are computationally much cheaper to calculate than a mean
averages cannot be graphed over time
Prometheus does not support computing averages
the average hides the slow tail that your unhappiest users experience
questions.explanation: A decent mean can coexist with a terrible p99 — tail percentiles show what the worst-served requests actually experience.

questions.text: An "exporter" in the Prometheus ecosystem is…
options: a job that ships metrics off to cold storage
the component that emails alert notifications
a bridge that exposes a third-party system's metrics in Prometheus format
a Grafana plugin used for exporting dashboard panels as printable PDF reports
questions.explanation: Exporters (node_exporter, blackbox_exporter, x509 exporters…) translate systems that don't speak Prometheus into scrapeable /metrics.

questions.text: In the Prometheus stack, Alertmanager is responsible for…
options: grouping, routing and silencing alerts on their way to receivers
continuously evaluating the alerting rules against the stored metric series
storing the alert history for compliance
drawing the alert panels inside Grafana
questions.explanation: Prometheus evaluates rules and fires alerts; Alertmanager dedupes, groups, routes (Slack, email, PagerDuty) and handles silences.

questions.text: Grafana dashboard provisioning (dashboards as code/config) is valuable because…
options: provisioned dashboards render measurably faster in the user's browser
dashboards survive rebuilds and are versioned instead of hand-edited
it is the only way to use template variables
it removes the need for a Prometheus datasource
questions.explanation: Provisioning from files (e.g. via a Helm chart sidecar watching ConfigMaps) makes dashboards reproducible, reviewable and restorable.

questions.text: An error budget is…
options: the cloud spend reserved each quarter for incident response
the count of bugs allowed per sprint
the acceptable amount of unreliability implied by your SLO
the maximum size of the on-call rota
questions.explanation: A 99.9% SLO implies ~43 minutes of failure per month; while budget remains you ship features, when it's spent you prioritise reliability.

questions.text: histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) computes…
options: an estimated p99 latency from histogram buckets
the exact worst request seen in 5 minutes
the total count of requests slower than 0.99 seconds
the 99th slowest endpoint by average latency
questions.explanation: It interpolates the 99th percentile from cumulative bucket counters — an estimate whose accuracy depends on bucket boundaries.

questions.text: The USE method for resource analysis checks…
options: uptime, stability, efficiency and overall responsiveness
utilisation, saturation and errors for every resource
user sessions and engagement
updates, security and encryption
questions.explanation: Brendan Gregg's USE method: for each resource (CPU, disk, network…) check utilisation, saturation and errors.

questions.text: The Pushgateway is intended for…
options: speeding up scrapes of slow targets
buffering alerts when Alertmanager is down
replacing standard exporters entirely on air-gapped internal networks
short-lived batch jobs that end before a scrape would reach them
questions.explanation: Batch jobs push their final metrics to the Pushgateway, which holds them for Prometheus to scrape; using it as a general proxy is an anti-pattern.

questions.text: OpenTelemetry (OTel) is best described as…
options: a vendor-neutral standard and SDKs for emitting traces, metrics and logs
Grafana's own proprietary tracing backend for enterprise-licensed customers
a Kubernetes controller for restarting slow pods
a log compression format for object storage
questions.explanation: OTel standardises instrumentation and wire formats so you can switch backends (Jaeger, Tempo, vendors) without re-instrumenting.

questions.text: A "burn rate" alert fires when…
options: the CPU temperature of a node crosses a threshold
the error budget is being consumed faster than the SLO window allows
the monthly cloud bill exceeds its agreed forecast by a configured margin
log volume grows faster than disk can absorb
questions.explanation: Burn-rate alerting (e.g. multiwindow multi-burn-rate) pages when you're spending error budget so fast the SLO is at risk.

questions.text: Sampling in tracing systems exists because…
options: regulations forbid tracing every user request
traces are only useful for failed requests
spans automatically expire after a fixed maximum number of network hops
recording every trace at scale costs more than the insight is worth
questions.explanation: Head- or tail-based sampling keeps a representative or interesting subset (e.g. errors, slow requests) to control cost at high volume.

questions.text: "Staleness" handling in Prometheus means that…
options: counters are automatically reset back to zero every 24 hours of continuous uptime
old dashboards are archived automatically
a series that stops being scraped soon stops returning data points in queries
alerts expire if not acknowledged within an hour
questions.explanation: When a target or series disappears, staleness markers stop queries returning its last value forever — important when pods come and go.

questions.text: The strongest argument for dashboards-as-code over hand-built ones is…
options: hand-built dashboards cannot use template variables
dashboards defined as code always execute their queries measurably faster at page load
Grafana licenses charge per manual edit
review, reuse and recovery: changes are diffable and environments stay consistent
questions.explanation: As-code dashboards go through PR review, can be stamped across environments and are trivially restored — hand edits drift and vanish.

questions.text: Monitoring TLS/x509 certificate expiry with an exporter matters because…
options: expired certificates cause outages that are entirely predictable and preventable
certificates cannot be renewed without a monitoring system
browsers now require cryptographic proof of active monitoring before trusting a certificate
expiry dates change dynamically and must be tracked hourly
questions.explanation: Cert expiry is one of the most preventable outage classes: an exporter plus a weeks-ahead alert turns a 2am outage into a routine ticket.
